sanskrit_devanagari:
नियतं कुरु कर्म त्वं कर्म ज्यायो ह्यकर्मणः ।
शरीरयात्रापि च ते न प्रसिद्ध्येदकर्मणः ॥ ८ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
niyataṁ kuru karma tvaṁ karma jyāyo hy akarmaṇaḥ |
śarīra-yātrāpi ca te na prasidhyed akarmaṇaḥ || 8 ||

translations.verbatim_quote: Perform your prescribed duty, for doing so is better than not working. One cannot even maintain one’s physical body without work.
Perform your prescribed duty, for doing so is better than not working. One cannot even maintain one’s physical body without work.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1311 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: There are many pseudo meditators who misrepresent themselves as belonging to high parentage, and great professional men who falsely pose that they have sacrificed everything for the sake of advancement in spiritual life. Lord Kṛṣṇa did not want Arjuna to become a pretender.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.8 Tvam, you, O Arjuna; kuru, perform; niyatam, the obligatory; karma, duties, those daily obligatory duties (nitya-karmas) or which one is competent (according to the scriptures), and which are not heard of [although no result of daily obligatory duties is mentioned in the scriptures, still Sankar…
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-8-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Advaita reading: niyata = nityam (regulated daily duty); the verse argues from necessity (without action, even bodily maintenance fails) to the value-claim (action is better than inaction). On this reading, this is the chapter's pragmatic floor: until the actor reaches jñāna-niṣṭhā, action is required AND superior to abstention. The reasoning is consequentialist (necessity) backed by metaphysics (action's purificatory function).

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 3.8 ‘Obligatory’ (Niyatam) means ‘concomitant’ (Vyaptam); for action is concomitant with that which is conjoined with Prakrti or the body. The contact with Prakrti has arisen from beginingless subtle impressions (Vasanas).
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-3-8-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: The Vishishtadvaita reading: niyata-karma covers obligatory (nitya) and occasional (naimittika) acts taught in the Veda. Their performance, freed from attachment, becomes the means of purification. The verse's pragmatic claim — even bodily maintenance fails without action — backs the metaphysical claim — action is better than inaction. The whole frame: do the prescribed action as worship; do not abstain.

positions.rendering: obligatory and occasional acts taught in the Veda

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: niyata literally = regulated, fixed, prescribed. The Advaita reading takes it as nitya-karma (daily obligatory action — the constant duty). The Vishishtadvaita reading takes it as covering both nitya (obligatory) and naimittika (occasional) acts. Modern translators say 'prescribed duty' which is general enough to be uncontroversial. The substantive point both ācāryas preserve: action is not arbitrary; what is endorsed is the duty that has already been assigned by one's role and station.

literal_meaning: Perform your prescribed (niyata) action — action is indeed better than inaction; even your bodily maintenance would not be possible without action.
traditional_meaning_consensus: 3.8 is the crisp directive. niyataṁ kuru karma tvam — perform your prescribed (regulated) action; karma jyāyo hy akarmaṇaḥ — action is better than inaction. The pragmatic floor: śarīra-yātrāpi ca te na prasidhyed akarmaṇaḥ — even your bodily maintenance would not succeed without action. Per the Advaita reading: niyata-karma = nityam, regulated daily duty; the verse argues from necessity to value. Per the Vishishtadvaita reading: prescribed action covers obligatory and occasional acts; their performance, freed from attachment, becomes the means. The verse is the chapter's most direct doctrinal statement: act, because action is necessary AND superior to inaction.
